benchmarks.honest_notes: QAOA found a marginally better discrete-subset solution than continuous Markowitz. This is the expected behavior on classical simulation. True quantum advantage requires fault-tolerant hardware.
VQE on classical simulation cannot beat exact diagonalization for n=3 qubits (8 states). Value is hardware-portability and architectural correctness.
Grover gives O(sqrt(N/M)) QUERY complexity (3 vs 16 classical). Wall-clock simulation is O(N) per iteration, so it does NOT beat linear scan on a CPU. Hardware-portable structure.
MLQAE on classical simulation provably cannot beat classical Monte Carlo wall-clock — each oracle query is O(2^n). Value is framing/correctness/hardware-readiness, not speedup.
Grover for arb detection: hardware-portable O(sqrt(N)) query complexity vs O(N) classical scan. Wall-clock on simulator is comparable since each iteration costs O(N).

global_honest_notes: All quantum algorithms in this suite run on the in-repo classical simulator. Quantum query complexity speedups are real on hardware but do NOT translate to wall-clock speedups in simulation. The value of these implementations is correctness, framing, and hardware-portability.
